body.title: Intentions are the ROOT of everything — the authority graph is intention → decision → file, and nothing is canonical that cannot trace back to one
body.author: Akien (decision) + CC.0 (capture)
body.text:
# D-intentions-are-the-root-2026-07-13

**Akien, 2026-07-13:** "Yes, intentions are the root of everything now."
And: "the intentions are the North Star. even though they can conflict with one another, as a body, once their conflicts are resolved, that's where we're going always."

## The claim

INTENTIONS ARE THE ROOT OF THE AUTHORITY GRAPH.

    intention  ->  decision  ->  file / code / canonicity claim

Everything derives its authority from an intention. A file, a rule, a 'canonical source' claim, a
design — anything with NO PATH BACK TO AN INTENTION is UNMOORED. It may still be correct, but it is
correct by luck, and nothing can check it.

## Why this is a decision and not a preference

A canonicity claim is fundamentally a claim about WHAT SERVES THE INTENT. Only the intentions say what
the intent IS. So a file that declares itself canonical without an intention behind it has asserted an
answer to a question nobody asked it — SELF-APPOINTED AUTHORITY, which is structurally identical to a
confabulation: a confident claim, no backing, believed by future readers PRECISELY BECAUSE it sounds
authoritative.

MEASURED 2026-07-13: **67 files in the tree assert canonicity. The store holds THREE intentions.**

## Intentions CONFLICT, and resolving the conflicts IS the work

Akien is explicit: intentions MAY conflict with one another. That is not a defect in the body of
intentions — it is the design surface. Two intentions in tension pose a question that has not been
answered yet, and an unanswered question about what we MEAN is exactly an OPEN QUESTION (see note
two-kinds-of-question-2026-07-13).

So the machine closes on itself:

    intentions conflict  ->  OPEN QUESTION  ->  DECISION  ->  GRANT  ->  canonicity

That chain IS the authority graph, and it is the same chain the compiler walks. An open question
COMPILES OUT into a decision; a decision GRANTS authority; a file POINTS AT its grant. Nothing
self-appoints anywhere along it.

## THE EPOCH BOUNDARY (this is what the '30 days' actually meant)

CC.0 first read Akien's '~30 days' as an AGE THRESHOLD and then, over-correcting, threw the number out
entirely. Both were wrong. It is an **EPOCH BOUNDARY**, and the distinction is operational:

  - An AGE THRESHOLD rolls forward. Every file becomes suspect on its 31st day, forever. The work never
    finishes and eventually flags everything, including files already reconciled.
  - An EPOCH BOUNDARY is a FIXED LINE. Everything authored before intentions existed has never been
    checked against one; everything after should have been. The set is FINITE and it SHRINKS as it is
    reconciled. The work COMPLETES.

The epoch, measured (not guessed):
  ~2026-06-13  Akien begins talking about intention (his '~30 days ago')
   2026-06-26  the concept lands in code — goals purged; `intentions` replaces the retired `goals` edge
               (T-skills-goals-to-intentions / D-goals-purge-remaining-skills-2026-06-26)
   2026-07-01  the FIRST intention is actually emitted (I-compiled-inference-residue)

Anything written before ~mid-June was authored in a world where intention was not the organizing idea.
Anything before 2026-07-01 COULD NOT have been checked against an intention even in principle — there
were none to check against.

## THE ORDER OF WORK INVERTS (the honest part — CP1)

You cannot check 67 canonicity claims against 3 intentions. The North Star is barely mapped.

Building the canonicity sweep FIRST would produce a GREEN CHECK THAT MEANS NOTHING — which is exactly
the passing-check-that-checks-nothing failure this decision exists to kill, and which bit us four times
on 2026-07-12 alone (questions.json's false canonicity claim, which fooled CC.0 into building a
duplicate home; the '51% MEASURED' headline that beat a correct memory; identity.home_db_url vs
db_proxy.make_home_proxy asserting opposite things about the same env var, with the employer-privacy
rule resting on that boundary; and 8 orphan skills invisible to every skills audit).

So:
  STEP 1  WRITE THE INTENTIONS DOWN. Not a tooling task. The North Star is mostly still in Akien's head.
          This is the prerequisite for every check that follows.
  STEP 2  Resolve the conflicts BETWEEN intentions (they will conflict — that is expected and is where
          the design actually happens).
  STEP 3  Trace each canonicity claim to an intention: it either EARNS a grant, or LOSES the claim.
  STEP 4  Fold the standing check into validity_sweep.py at day-close (no new tool).

## Consequences

- Every NEW canonicity claim must cite its grant at birth. No self-appointment.
- `/sorted` and the decision flow should carry an `intention` edge — a decision with no parent intention
  is a decision about nothing in particular.
- The 3-intention store is now the single biggest gap in the project, and it is NOT a code gap.
- FALSIFIABLE PREDICTION: as the intentions get written, some existing 'canonical' files will turn out to
  serve NO intention. Those are not mis-filed — they are DEAD, and their existence is the evidence.

## Related

I-compiled-inference-residue · I-self-improving-process · I-reader-reads-an-address-into-domain-trees

validity_conditions.target: The body of intentions is rich enough to check claims against
validity_conditions.note: TODAY IT IS NOT — 3 intentions vs 67 canonicity claims. Every check downstream of this decision is meaningless until that changes. If a canonicity sweep is ever reported GREEN while the intention count is still tiny, the sweep is lying.
